data.title: SunPower sinks after disclosing material errors in prior 2025 quarterly results
data.description:
SunPower (SPWR) down 6.1% in early trading Wednesday after filing its audited 2025 Form 10-K, which highlighted that GAAP revenue totaled $300M, down from $308.75M previously reported due to the removal of double bookings, and a ~$26.9M GAAP operating loss following a one-time balance sheet cleanup.

After standard adjustments, SunPower (SPWR) said FY 2025 non-GAAP operating income totaled nearly $7.33M.

SunPower (SPWR) said it plans to restate its Q1, Q2, and Q3 2025 quarterly reports to align with the 10-K and stated a 2026 plan targeting more than $400K in revenues.

The company said material errors were related to the recognition of revenue—and related cost of revenues, sales commissions, sales and marketing, and general and administrative expenses—and interest expense.

SunPower (SPWR) said the material errors were the result of previously reported material weaknesses in its internal control over financial reporting related to its control activities, information and communication, and monitoring activities.

data.title: PepsiCo Q1 earnings in focus amid pricing reset
data.description:
PepsiCo (PEP) will report its results for the first quarter on Thursday, before markets open.

Wall Street expects the company to post earnings of $1.55, implying a rise of +4.7%, on revenue of $18.94B, representing year-over-year growth of about 6%.

During the quarter, PepsiCo operated against a backdrop of rising pressures across the consumer packaged goods sector, with evolving demand trends and competitive dynamics shaping its strategy. The company responded to consumer pushback by lowering prices on key snack brands such as Lay’s and Doritos, while continuing to emphasize affordability, which helped drive shelf space gains and support North American growth.

At the same time, PepsiCo leaned into innovation and marketing, including launching a prebiotic soda nationally, expanding the Poppi brand internationally, and tapping social media influencers to boost engagement. The group also advanced its digital and operational initiatives through AI deployments in China and collaborations with industrial partners, while mitigating geopolitical risks via local sourcing and hedging strategies.

Additionally, PepsiCo navigated legal scrutiny over its pricing practices, securing a U.S. court order to block a proposed class action, even as broader industry trends, including the impact of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, prompted shifts toward product reformulation and smaller pack sizes.

According to Alpha’s Quant Rating system, PEP is rated Buy with an overall score of 4.37 out of 5, reflecting an A+ grade in terms of profitability, but it has a D+ in terms of valuation.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis said PepsiCo is undergoing  structural changes aimed at improving efficiency and supporting growth, pointing out that, “Pepsi commented that they're slashing their total number of U.S. SKUs by ~20% during 1H 2026, which should optimize the firm's DSD (Direct-Store-Delivery) network and remove unnecessary manufacturing complexity.”

Over the past two years, PEP has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 63% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen three upward revisions and nine downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen seven upward revisions and one downward move.

The stock has gained nearly 7.3% so far this year, compared to a 1.78% rise in the S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Mar Vista U.S. Quality Premier adds Ecolab, GE Vernova, QXO, exits Salesforce, SAP
data.description:
• Mar Vista's U.S. Quality Premier strategy returned -10.17% net-of-fees in the first quarter of 2026.
  • During the quarter, it initiated new investments in Ecolab (ECL), GE Vernova (GEV), and QXO Inc (QXO).
  • It also exited positions in Salesforce (CRM) and SAP ADR (SAP). During the quarter-end rebalance of the Quality Premier strategy, the fund also added to its holdings in Danaher (DHR), Netflix (NFLX), and Linde plc (LIN) and trimmed Intuit (INTU) and Meta Platforms (META).
  • "We believe the market is transitioning into a more fundamentally driven environment where returns could be more closely tied to earnings durability, free cash flow generation, and capital discipline."

data.title: Senator Warren sees 'risks' to Nvidia's SchedMD deal in letter to Hegseth, Wright
data.description:
Senator Elizabeth Warren said she sees “risks” to Nvidia's (NVDA) acquisition of open-source software company SchedMD in a letter to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of Energy Chris Wright.

“I write to express concern about NVIDIA’s recent acquisition of SchedMD and request information about the government’s dependency on NVIDIA hardware and software,” Warren wrote in a letter to Secys. Hegseth and Wright. “This acquisition raises particular alarm due to the Department of Energy (DOE) and Department of Defense’s (DoD) widespread reliance on SchedMD’s workload management software, Slurm. Slurm software is used in critical DOE and DoD supercomputers and is 'the top software used worldwide in aerospace and defense.' NVIDIA’s acquisition of Slurm turns a once free software into one of NVIDIA’s proprietary offerings, which may reduce competition and harm national security. This would give NVIDIA disproportionate control over a chokepoint that rival firms rely on to operate government supercomputers.”

In her letter to the Cabinet members, Sen. Warren asked for additional information on which DOE and DOD systems rely on Nvidia's software or hardware. She has requested answers by May 5.

Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Led by CEO Jensen Huang, Nvidia announced in December 2025 it was acquiring SchedMD, maker of the popular open-source Slurm (Simple Linux Utility Resource Management) software. Slurm functions as a manager and job scheduler for high-performance computing clusters, helping sets of computers function as a single system.

Nvidia said it plans to continue developing and distributing Slurm as an open-source, vendor-neutral software solution. Slurm is already used by more than half of the top 10 and top 100 systems in the TOP500 list of supercomputers. Slurm is also supported by Nvidia's most powerful hardware. The two companies have collaborated for more than a decade.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: GTLB, AVGO, SNAP, DOO, and more
data.description:
Stock futures stayed cautious Wednesday as traders watched for potential new U.S.-Iran peace talks this week while tracking a heavy slate of corporate earnings reports.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Eos Energy (EOSE) +15% - Shares jumped after announcing a partnership with TURBINE-X Energy to develop private power infrastructure for AI data centers, with plans to deploy up to 2 GWh of storage systems over the next three years. The collaboration targets large-scale, multi-hundred-megawatt projects and underscores growing demand for fast, reliable energy solutions to support AI-driven infrastructure buildouts.
  • Snap (SNAP) +6% - Shares climbed after announcing plans to cut ~1,000 jobs (~16% of workforce) and eliminate 300+ open roles as part of a restructuring aimed at driving profitable growth, with CEO Evan Spiegel citing AI-driven efficiencies. The company also guided Q1 revenue to ~$1.53B, slightly above consensus, and adjusted EBITDA of ~$233M, reinforcing improving fundamentals.
  • Morgan Stanley (MS) +5% - Shares edged up after Q1 results beat expectations, with EPS of $3.43 and revenue of $20.6B driven by strong trading activity and continued momentum in wealth management. Institutional securities revenue surged on robust equity and fixed income trading, while wealth management delivered solid asset flows, helping lift margins, though net interest income missed estimates and credit provisions increased, tempering some optimism.
  • Broadcom (AVGO) +4% - Shares rose after announcing a multi-year AI partnership with Meta Platforms ((META)) to develop and deploy custom MTIA chips across data centers, with an initial commitment exceeding 1 GW and a broader multi-generation rollout planned through 2029. The deal reinforces Broadcom’s positioning in AI infrastructure and custom silicon while deepening its role in Meta’s long-term compute strategy.
  • GitLab (GTLB) +4% - Shares advanced
  • after the company expanded its partnership with Google Cloud, enabling customers to integrate GitLab’s Duo Agent Platform with Vertex AI models, with usage counting toward existing cloud commitments. The collaboration strengthens GitLab’s positioning in AI-driven software development by combining its end-to-end DevSecOps platform with advanced AI capabilities, enhancing its role in the evolving agent-based development ecosystem.
  • Stellantis (STLA) +4% - Shares gained after reporting an estimated ~12% Y/Y increase in Q1 global shipments to 1.36M units, driven by broad-based strength across regions. North America led with a 17% gain on strong demand for key models, while Europe, Asia Pacific, and other regions also posted solid growth supported by new vehicle launches and platform-driven momentum, signaling resilient demand across its global portfolio.

Biggest stock losers

  • BRP (DOO) -35% – Shares plunged after the company withdrew its FY2027 outlook following changes to U.S. Section 232 tariffs, which now impose a 25% levy on total vehicle value instead of a 50% duty on select metal content. The shift is expected to drive over $500M in incremental costs this year, creating significant uncertainty, with CEO Denis Le Vot flagging a volatile tariff environment despite plans to mitigate the impact.
  • TeraWulf (WULF) -7% - Shares fell after pricing an upsized $900M equity offering, raising dilution concerns, alongside softer-than-expected preliminary Q1 results with revenue of $30M–$35M below consensus and adjusted EBITDA to be between $0M and $3M. The company said proceeds will fund data center expansion, debt repayment, and new site development, but the combination of capital raise and weak near-term performance weighed on sentiment.

data.title: CoreWeave lands $6B deal to provide Jane Street with AI cloud services
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) landed a $6B deal with Jane Street to provide AI cloud computing services to the global technology-driven trading firm.

Under the new agreement, CoreWeave will provide Jane Street with access to compute across multiple facilities, including Nvidia's (NVDA) Vera Rubin technology and the software and services required to deploy and scale AI solutions. Jane Street also made a $1B investment in CoreWeave's Class A common stock at a purchase price of $109 per share.

"We are deeply committed to investing in cutting-edge technologies that support our research in global financial markets, training large, complex models on massive volumes of noisy data, refining them continuously, and deploying at a scale to help make markets more efficient," said Jane Street.

"Jane Street operates like a frontier lab, continually breaking new ground in deep learning and pushing the scale and complexity of their models," said Max Hjelm, senior vice president of revenue at CoreWeave. "CoreWeave was built for this purpose, and we're excited to expand our collaboration with Jane Street."

For CoreWeave, this follows up on recent, multi-billion-dollar deals the AI hyperscaler has made with Meta (META) and Anthropic (ANTHRO).  

CoreWeave shares were down 2% during early market action on Wednesday, but the stock has surged 37% over the past month.

data.title: China weighs curbs on exports of solar manufacturing equipment to U.S. - Reuters
data.description:
Chinese officials have held initial talks with solar panel equipment manufacturers, as the government considers limiting exports of the industry's most advanced technology to the U.S., Reuters reported Wednesday.

China, which is estimated to make more than 80% of the world's ‌solar panel components, is home to the top 10 suppliers of equipment to make solar cells.

If adopted, such a move could threaten plans by U.S. firms to build new factories or expand existing plants in efforts to boost local production, and widen export controls in another area of technology where China has a lead, building on its efforts to control rare earth exports.

Analysts who track China's solar industry have braced for export controls, in part because concern is growing over efforts by Tesla's (TSLA) Elon Musk and others to boost solar panel production in the U.S., reducing reliance on China.

Tesla (TSLA) "succeeding in its solar self-sufficiency push could prove a nightmare for China's world-leading solar manufacturers," research firm Trivium China said in a note earlier this month. "Beijing won't sit idly by as its industrial champions inadvertently aid the industrial policies of rival countries."

Potentially relevant solar stocks include First Solar (FSLR), Enphase Energy (ENPH), SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG), Array Technologies (ARRY), Shoals Technologies (SHLS), Sunrun (RUN), JinkoSolar (JKS), Canadian Solar (CSIQ), Daqo New Energy (DQ).

ETF: (TAN)

data.title: Oakmark Select Fund lags S&P 500 in Q1, adds Gartner and Marsh & McLennan, exits APA and Molina
data.description:
• Oakmark Select Fund (Investor Class) underperformed the benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, for the quarter, but outperformed since inception.
  • The fund added Gartner (IT) and Marsh & McLennan (MRSH); exited APA (APA) and Molina Healthcare (MOH).
  • The fund sees equity markets as increasingly influenced by short-term noise and crowd behavior rather than fundamentals, amid elevated volatility, geopolitical headlines, and wide stock dispersion.

data.title: OpenAI mulls new pricing and potential upgrades for ChatGPT ads: report
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) is planning to start pricing some ChatGPT ads based on if people click on the ads rather than just how many people see them, The Information reported, citing an agency executive who spoke with the AI startup's staff and works with ChatGPT advertisers.

In addition, OpenAI has indicated it plans to introduce ads aimed at getting people to take a specific action, like making a purchase or downloading an app, said the agency executive, though the company has not put a firm timeline on when that could happen, the report added.

OpenAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The discussions indicate OpenAI’s efforts to make ChatGPT more appealing for marketers as it chases its ambitious growth targets and looks to compete with digital ad giants Meta Platforms (META) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

Earlier this month, it was reported that OpenAI expects to generate about $2.5B in advertising revenue this year, with projections rising to as much as $100B annually by 2030. The company's ad pilot program in the U.S. had generated over $100M in annualized revenue in six weeks of launch.

The AI giant, which began showing ads in ChatGPT in early February, has so far only provided early advertisers with aggregated data like impressions, clicks, and spend. Meta and Google, by contrast, provide additional information on who sees ads and if they lead to purchases, the report noted.

Marketers have noted that it is hard to tell if ChatGPT’s early ads are working and likely need stronger tracking and measurement tools from OpenAI before they will move beyond small, experimental ad spending. Offering pricing on a per-click basis would let advertisers pay when users engage with an ad, while conversion-oriented ad campaigns may let them optimize spending toward actions like buying, according to the report.

"A lot of clients who would have the budget to want to test on a new surface are holding back and are tentative because they don’t think they’re going to be able to get the measurement that they want out of it," said Ben Kahan, head of the programmatic practice at marketing agency Brainlabs.

OpenAI began testing ads in early February for logged-in U.S. users of the free and lowest-priced version of ChatGPT, but until now the rollout has been slow. The Microsoft (MSFT)-backed company has extended the pilot period for its earliest advertisers beyond the end of March because it has not shown ads often enough to exhaust the initial spending commitments, the report added, citing two people managing ChatGPT ads on behalf of advertisers.

OpenAI is also selling some ads for less than it initially planned. The company targeted a cost per thousand impressions, or cost per mille, up to $60, on par with premium ads like those on streaming TV. But one media buyer working with some of the early ChatGPT advertisers said some of their clients had paid CPMs ranging from $15 to $25, likely indicating limited competition for ad inventory, the report added.

Last month, OpenAI hired one of Meta's former top digital advertising executives, David Dugan, to serve as its vice president of global ad solutions. Reportedly, OpenAI had held early talks to partner with The Trade Desk (TTD) to help it sell advertising.

An OpenAI spokesperson told the news outlet that the company is exploring different approaches to understand what creates real value for users and advertisers and will continue to iterate.

OpenAI told investors in the first quarter that it expects $11B in ad revenue in 2027. If it meets next year’s target, that would make it far larger than ad sellers such as Snap and Pinterest.

In recent weeks, OpenAI employees have indicated to marketers their intention to make the ChatGPT ad offering more competitive with the big digital ad companies. OpenAI has told some advertisers that it will allow them to run ad campaigns that charge advertisers based on the number of clicks their ads get within the coming days, the report added, citing the agency executive who talked to OpenAI employees. That offering would be alongside its existing model that charges based on how often ads are shown.

OpenAI has discussed setting up ways for advertisers to run more action-oriented ads in the near future, but how fast it could roll out these capabilities is less clear. That expansion is also likely to require OpenAI to build or partner on tools to better track what ChatGPT users do after seeing an ad, such as tracking if they go on to make a buy.

In recent weeks, the company has already started giving some advertisers access to a portal where they can manage ad campaigns themselves, the report added, citing two people managing ads on behalf of advertisers. This is an upgrade from the manual processes involving spreadsheets and phone calls it had relied on earlier. It has also partnered with outside firms, including advertising technology company Criteo, which helps sell ads on its behalf.

OpenAI has begun to sign up some advertisers on a month-to-month basis, asking for monthly commitments in the range of $30,000 to $50,000, said Jai Amin, chief solutions officer at marketing agency Jellyfish, which has clients purchasing ChatGPT ads. This is a move from OpenAI’s earliest deals, where it asked for up-front commitments of at least $200,000 to spend by the end of the first quarter, according to the report.

OpenAI advertisers still see other areas for improvement. For one, they have limited control over where their ads appear. Currently, instead of trying to line up ads with specific searches or types of users in ChatGPT, OpenAI is asking advertisers for broad keywords to provide guidance on where it makes sense to show their ads, the report noted, citing agency executives. Since each ChatGPT response is unique, it is difficult for advertisers to ensure their ads will show up in response to certain prompts, even if the answers reference topics advertisers have listed.

In addition, the ChatGPT ads' appearance, which currently includes short headlines and a small image, is also basic compared to digital ads available elsewhere, the report added.

data.title: CME Group expands Equity Index Dividend suite
data.description:
CME Group (CME) on Wednesday announced plans to expand its Equity Index Dividend suite on May 11.

The derivatives marketplace is adding Mid-curve options on S&P 500 Annual Dividend Index futures, alongside Nasdaq-100 and Russell 2000 Quarterly Dividend Index futures.

"Trading in our dividend products has increased more than 50% year-over-year," said Global Head of Equity Products Joe Hickey.
